speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two, Albert Lichtmann. You were talking about your teachers at the gymnasium, gymnasium.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to say anything else about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Speaking about them and speaking about the school, it was our home away from home in a, in a, a very honest and serious manner. Because school hours ended at two o'clock in the afternoon or later. And often, we returned for other activities-- sports, which was very important in the school. And, and many of our students are very excellent sportsmen, achievers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and I, I was very much involved in all the sports, never anybody who had really done very well, except in the later years, when I was involved in ice hockey. And for a while, I was a goalie in a water polo junior team. But those activities were mostly outside of the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you participate in any other activities besides sports at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was part of the choir, singing in, in, in a religious choir, which, which, was part of the weekly Shabbos services. And I enjoyed it very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you sing a song from-- that you sang?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would rather hold that for a little longer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a song that you would sing, that you would sing?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the religious songs, which, which are for the Shabbos service or the high holiday service. As a matter of fact, I just went to a wonderful holiday service here in the Washington area yesterday, being the second day of Rosh Hashanah. And the cantor is a tremendous cantor, who's chanting I enjoy very much. And many of the songs of chantings of him and the choir were reminding me of the days when I was in that religious school choir myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're not prepared to sing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I, I would rather hold back from that. My voice was mediocre. It wasn't a soloist voice and probably deteriorated since then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- can you described any incidence of anti-Semitism that you experienced in school, perhaps before the gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, anti-Semitism was rampant in Hungary. It was not just because the Germans and Hitler came to power. But it was even before that. And as European history goes, which I don't think we should go too deep into it, there was anti-Semitism from the Middle Ages all the way to leading up to the Nazi times. And Hungary, indeed, was very much part of that. Hungarian Jewish population, relatively speaking to the size of the country, was a large Jewish community. Hungarian Jews were close to 6%, 7% of the total population of the country and, and had a very strong influence on the culture and the cultural life of Hungary. And they were successful in, in, in many areas of daily life, business, and, and, and culture, and theater, and newspapers, and writings, and so forth. And it was not taken very well by the other people who always started to push back and, and, and curtail the achievements of the Jews. Also, for example, some of the so-called anti-Jewish laws-- and there were several of them-- started to push back the number of admissions to the universities. The numerus clausus, closure on the number of how many Jewish people can be admitted to universities were every three, four years were pushed back and pushed back further and, and, and minimized. Why those people who were in universities, they were constantly attacked by very rightist movements, young students of their own classes. And there are documented atrocities against--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish school people. I do know it also from a, from a first hand experience in my, in my year when I was, I think, 12 or going on 13 when our school building on [INAUDIBLE] street, the Budapest Jewish gymnasium school, was attacked physically by a stone and brick-throwing group of people. And I was in a gymnastic class at the time. I vividly remember when somebody was rushing in and said that the school is attacked. And then the gymnasium was under the stone throwing. And they called for police protection. But also, to fight it off, many of the older students went out. And they just were fighting with physical fist fights and with all kind of things they could get their hands on to fight off this. And it was on a school, being in class, all the different classes, sitting and studying. And we were attacked by just this riotous hordes of older group of students or whoever incited them into that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had that first, firsthand experience. And classroom windows were broken into. Our classroom window was broken into and a, a big stone was hurled through that window. When you went home that evening, did you tell your parents about what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely. Definitely. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what they said to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't remember word by word. But I'm sure they were happy that none of us got seriously injured. But it was indeed one of the firsthand experience at age 12 of what anti-Semitism was like in my little life at the time. And, and from then on, the anti-Jewish laws which curtailed Jews' achievements financially, just like my father's license for doing business and many others, are well-documented facts which I think would be to detailed to go into it this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me. After school, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got home, I did some studying, taking my homework. And then I was looking for some activities. Those days, we didn't have televisions. Occasionally, I got to the movie theaters. And, and I went over to my friend's home nearby. And we have been playing. Interestingly, a little game which was very, very important to us then-- soccer was the sport in Europe. Football, as we called it. But it's soccer as it's known here. On a table or a table top, we were imitating the game using little buttons. And, and that was all over in Hungary. Everybody was playing soccer on it with little buttons to achieve the same achievement. Like little buttons were moving forward like balls and so on, hours and hours, and things like ping pong.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that game?

speaker: interviewee
text: Button soccer game on the table. It-- every European child knows it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and you were saying other--

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and nobody's winter coat with big buttons was, was safe from us. Because we were snatching them off if we could-- terrible thing to do. Because we glued them together and filed them that they could be placed on our team.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you were talking about other activities and other games you played.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, other activities-- ice skating-- I had a neighborhood who had the ice skating rink where, where I was going in the wintertime. In summertime, I usually went to visit my grandmother and uncle in that town which I mentioned previously.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your neighbor, the name of your neighbor that had the skating rink?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was not a neighbor of ours. That was in public skating rink. You had to buy your season tickets or your daily tickets. And sometime, I was just looking through the fence, because I didn't have money to get in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, classmates and neighborhood children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you say tell--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Jewish people, because there was not much interest among the Gentile neighbors of ours to have my friendship.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of some of your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there is a friend I remember well. At age 10, we attended the same, same elementary school from eight-- six to 10-- whose name is Miklos Mezei. He survived. And as a matter of fact, I seen him just a year ago on our visit in Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what you were like as a young man.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which, which years we talking about-- age-- I was at what age which you are asking?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would see that I was happy, more or less well-adjusted as I look as an adult on my years of growing up, more well-- more, more or less well-adjusted because I had a loving home. I always had love at home. My parents were always very supportive. And so were relatives, cousins, and uncles who were alive those days. And, and I was just nothing unusual, just an average young kid growing up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you like about yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I like about myself then? I would not say that I, I can point out anything. Especially, I like that I was at least a little high-- taller than the average, which was always in school, you stood up in line and the taller were and the shorter were in different direction and so on. And I was always a little better than the middle. So I liked my height, if you're talking about physical appearance. I, I liked that, that talking about age 16, that I was noticed by the girls. And, and I was-- I cannot bring any special things that I did or didn't like otherwise very much about myself. I was an average student. And I was very happy with my extra school activities like playing soccer and, and having fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you want to be like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if I had a real role model, I would say that yes and no. I didn't have a real role model. I admired-- I admired a relative from a distance who was a very successful industrialist in the United States. And as a child, I always had to write letters, or at least join the letters of my parents to this lady in the United States who was somebody in the distance who is very important to my parents. And I had to write letters to them. And her husband was a very successful industrialist. So in some degree, when I finished high school and I entered a mechanic training in a, in a company which was the telephone manufacturing company-- but telephone was just a name. And they had many other aspects of the manufacturing business, such as railroad station signaling systems, and, and movie projectors, and so on. I thought that someday I might be an industrialist myself. But this-- these were childish idealist visions of hope and so on. And those changed during the years to follow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he-- his name was John Polachek. And even so, if I never met him really-- yes, I did meet him, as a matter of fact. I was a young boy when they visited in Hungary. So I know I met him, although I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the extent to which Jewish ritual and law were observed in your home before the occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father and mother were observing Jews. And a step more than that-- my father was an Orthodox Jew, but an enlightened Orthodox Jew, if I could categorize it. His appearance was very assimilated. He didn't wear typical garb which was-- some of the country and, and religious Jews were known for and overemphasized. Because all the anti-Semitic cartoons and papers, they always showed Jews who looked totally different and dressed totally different, heavily bearded and sideburns hanging, the pious curling, and so forth. And they always showed them in a very ugly scene. And, and my family was very different. Looking in the back to Father's religious beliefs, he was an Orthodox man, a God-fearing man, who requested and we kept kosher home at home. And even so, if after World War II when I returned from concentration camp and I grew up, I kind of had questions about the importance of that. I always observed it. I ate kosher at home. And in my life outside of home, I was like anybody else. But I remember father as a very observant Jew. Every single day of his life, he was putting on the, the [? fill-in, ?] which is the [INAUDIBLE] as it's known in English and, and putting on his tallits for praying. And I recall him as somebody whose belief in God and his strengths through that has had a lot of influence on me as a growing child. And I would see that maybe in many ways helped my hopes and my, my survivor, survival in the concentration camp times, because his religious belief that God is with us gave a very mystical, strong strength to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Talk about the religious observances you remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had beautiful holidays, even so if-- even so if going to the synagogue was not necessarily the, the most beautiful walk. Because towards the years at age 14 to 16, before the Jews were even further restricted in Hungary, carrying our prayer book to the synagogue and carrying our, our prayer shawl, my father was hiding it in a brown bag that people on the street wouldn't know that there goes a Jew to the synagogue. Because atrocities on streets and remarks about that were frequent. And oftentime, I, I was proudly wearing my high school cap which had a little emblem on the front. And I was very proud of that little emblem. So was every other high school student of-- not member of the Jewish high school-- the different high schools. On the other hand, that was a dead giveaway that I was Jewish and I belonged to a Jewish school.

speaker: interviewee
text: So going to synagogue, if I went by myself, or going to-- on the streets to school, I often took it off just that I would not go through a neighborhood which I knew that people were out there to get us. And we had often been attacked. And when you earlier asked questions about the classmate of mine or classmates of mine, I recall one incident when we were-- after school-- going home together, this classmate of mine and myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I felt very safe always in his presence. He was a fighter. He could, he could beat up everybody, even if it was a number of years older than him. His name is Amicham Reichman. He's alive. He's in Israel. And he was part of the Israeli military establishment. And when I talked to him last time, I was calling in his memory this incident when he was the one who fought with me, fighting off this attack. We were attacked by so-called Hitlerjugends. And Hitlerjugend, was in Hungary, an organization which was a German organization. But many of the young boys in that organization were German backgrounds or Germans from Germany placed in Hungary. That organization was a very strong, major organization of Nazi teachings and background. And they were like above everybody else. And that's the group we faced, the two of us at the time, when I recall this attack on the street. We, we left that scene with bruises and bleeding nose, but so did they.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened? What do you remember happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just a major fight by 16 and 18-year-old children. The other group was older than us. And we were then called into the director's office of our Budapest Jewish high school, because the organization called on us that we have done some major attack on them. Imagine, the two of us on some six, seven of them. It wasn't-- I was never that strong. But Amicham did his share.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Describe how you celebrated your favorite religious holiday.

speaker: interviewee
text: Favorite holiday, favorite holidays-- one of the most favorite always to me was Passover and the Seder nights. Because again, it's mystical power of Jews being saved through freedom. It give us and to me a kind of a positive feeling about that, that we are to survive. Because we did survive the slavery in Egypt. So we will survive this horrible times as well. And my mother cooked and prepared for Seder nice. We always had friends and relatives at our table. And it was a wonderful holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember the Passover before the occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, many of them, but especially one which was not so beautiful. It was beautiful because we were still together. But that time was very close to-- it was in the beginning of 1944, the spring of 1944. And time during the celebration, which was only the three of us at the table-- my mother, my father, and myself-- the sirens were sounded in the city. And we all had to go down to the shelter of the building, the cellar. But that was the air raid shelter. And as much as we welcomed the air raids, because that meant that the Americans the bombing, we were fearing for our life. So our Passover dinner was interrupted. But the building was shaking because nearby locations were bombed. And then my father went up after the siren sounded that the air attack was over. And he said, now we finish the Seder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: The bar mitzvah, age 13-- our Budapest Jewish gymnasium had a very nice synagogue temple in the building which was adorned with beautiful stained glass windows. As a child, I adored them. And I loved them. And when my bar mitzvah was, we had about six of us at the same day bar mitzvah. And I was one of them. And as the usual routine goes that the portion of the Haftorah is to be chanted or, or read by the bar mitzvah boy, the bar mitzvah man. At age 13, that's when your manhood starts. We had to share the [INAUDIBLE]. We were six of us, so probably the one who was quite good in the reading of the Haftorah was given that portion. And the rest of us read a portion from the Torah. But nevertheless, it was a nice event with six of us being there and our families and friends. And after that, we had a-- not the, the big parties which are now done in the United States. And we did the same for my daughters as being bat mitzvahed. But it was a much more modest celebration. Nevertheless, some family members came. And, and we received gifts like a young boy does it here, too. And we received the fountain pen. And we received the candies. It was a very happy day. Later on, we can even see the invitation which my parents send out for the bar mitzvah of mine. Because somehow, it survived World War II. And if I, if I am correct, I think how it got back to my hand is that a relative of ours, of ours here in the United States handed it over to me after I came to the United States at our meeting here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of any of the other boys that were bar mitzvahed with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember Gabor Alpar. I remember Jan, Janos Neumann. And that's about, I think-- the other four, or one, I was one of them. The other three, I can't recall right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Talk about any contact you had with Jewish refugees fleeing Nazi aggression.

speaker: interviewee
text: During the time as I was in high school, I was aware that there were Zionist activities within the school-- very minimal, very minimal. Our school principal and teachers were not in favor of that. I kind of reflected that a little bit, that they didn't want us to stand up for ourselves. They want us to survive kind of quietly and not to fight. And they thought that that's the approach you might survive. On the other hand, according some, it was a mistake. Because a tremendous number of our class perished as well as tremendous number of the Jewish population perished. But I am not aware of people who were able to get out of the country, although it's known and documented that there were very few, but some.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your teachers teach you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did they teach us not to be fighting? By not teaching us to stand up and fight, except we knew for one or two of the teachers who had, who had different views who a little bit spoke of, of the days when, when Jews can have dignity again. But it was not the school's curricula to, to make us standing up for ourself as Jews, just fit in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your struggles and conflicts?

speaker: interviewee
text: What were the struggles?

speaker: interviewer
text: Struggles and conflicts-- what did you worry about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Struggles and conflicts-- well, I had plenty. Because as a young boy growing up, I could never understand what was my sin. What's wrong with somebody who is Jewish? I, I had classmates in the elementary school who were, who were Catholics, who were Protestants. And, and I was Jewish. So I kind of understood that there are other religions. But mine was singled out to be the wrong one. And as, as I grew up, that always gave me a, a lot of questions in my mind about what and why people are anti-Semitic. So these questions in some way are still around. And anti-Semitism didn't die, even today. But on the other hand, hopefully it never will reach the proportions which happened under and during World War II with the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll change tapes. Is that OK?
